Russia shares land borders with both Norway and North Korea, on opposite ends of the same country.
Point Nemo in the Pacific is so far from land that the nearest humans are often astronauts passing overhead on the space station.
Africa is the only continent that stretches into all four hemispheres, north, south, east, and west.
Kiribati is the only country whose territory sits in all four hemispheres at once.
Canada contains more lakes than every other country on Earth combined.
Australia is wider from east to west than the Moon is across.
Alaska is both the westernmost and easternmost state in America, because the Aleutian Islands cross the 180th meridian.
Reno, Nevada sits farther west than Los Angeles, despite Nevada being the inland state.
Maine is the closest American state to Africa, not Florida.
France's longest land border is with Brazil, thanks to French Guiana in South America.
China spans five geographical time zones but runs the entire country on Beijing time.
Nepal's clocks are offset by 45 minutes from the usual hour marks, one of only a few such time zones on Earth.
Parts of Kiribati use the earliest time zone on the planet, a full 14 hours ahead of universal time.
Vatican City is so small that about eight of it would fit inside New York's Central Park.
Measured from the center of the Earth, Ecuador's Mount Chimborazo beats Everest, because the planet bulges at the equator.
Mauna Kea in Hawaii is taller than Everest when measured from its base on the sea floor, over ten kilometers of mountain.
The shore of the Dead Sea is the lowest dry land on Earth, about 430 meters below sea level.
Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench is deeper than Mount Everest is tall, with room to spare.
Angel Falls in Venezuela drops nearly a full kilometer, the tallest waterfall on Earth.
Lake Baikal in Siberia holds around one fifth of all the unfrozen fresh water on the planet.
Baikal is also the world's deepest lake, plunging more than 1,600 meters down.
Despite its name, the Caspian Sea is actually the largest lake in the world.
Antarctica is technically the largest desert on Earth, far bigger than the Sahara.
Weather stations in Chile's Atacama Desert have gone decades without recording a single drop of rain.
Around 60 percent of the world's fresh water is locked up in the ice sheet covering Antarctica.
Bir Tawil, a patch of desert between Egypt and Sudan, is claimed by neither country.
In the town of Baarle on the Belgian Dutch border, the boundary runs straight through houses and cafes.
A library in Derby Line straddles the border, with its books in Canada and its front door in the United States.
The two Diomede Islands are less than four kilometers apart, yet 21 hours apart on the clock.
Finland and North Korea are separated by just one country: Russia.
Spain shares land borders with Morocco, through its two small cities on the African coast.
Mount Everest grows a few millimeters taller every year as India keeps pushing into Asia.
Rock at the summit of Everest is marine limestone, formed at the bottom of an ancient sea.
Just a few thousand years ago the Sahara was green, dotted with lakes, hippos, and fishing villages.
Dust blown from the Sahara crosses the Atlantic Ocean and fertilizes the Amazon rainforest.
The Amazon pours more water into the sea than the next several biggest rivers combined.
Not a single bridge crosses the main stem of the Amazon River along its entire length.
Iceland has no mosquitoes at all, one of the very few places on Earth without them.
Iceland's parliament was founded in the year 930, making it one of the oldest in the world.
La Rinconada in Peru sits above 5,000 meters, the highest permanent settlement on Earth.
Australia built the city of Canberra as its capital because Sydney and Melbourne could not stop arguing over the title.
Nauru is a country with no official capital city at all.
Liechtenstein and Uzbekistan are the only countries surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries.
No point in Lesotho lies below 1,400 meters, the highest low point of any nation.
Lesotho, San Marino, and Vatican City are each completely surrounded by a single other country.
Point Roberts in Washington State can only be reached by land by driving through Canada.
Residents of Minnesota's Northwest Angle must drive through Canada to reach the rest of their own state by road.
Longyearbyen in the Arctic stopped burials long ago, because bodies simply do not decompose in the frozen ground.
On Svalbard, anyone leaving town is required to carry protection against polar bears, and most carry rifles.
Monaco is smaller than New York's Central Park, yet it is one of the richest places on the planet.
Greenland is the largest island on Earth, if you agree that Australia counts as a continent.
On common world maps Greenland looks the size of Africa, but Africa is actually about 14 times larger.
Africa is so vast that the United States, China, India, and most of Europe could fit inside it together.
Canada has the longest coastline of any country, over 200,000 kilometers of it.
Close to 90 percent of all humans live in the Northern Hemisphere.
The strongest earthquake ever recorded struck Chile in 1960, at magnitude 9.5.
Furnace Creek in Death Valley holds the official record for hottest air temperature ever measured, nearly 57 degrees Celsius.
The coldest temperature ever recorded on Earth was minus 89 degrees Celsius, at Vostok Station in Antarctica.
Oymyakon in Siberia is the coldest permanently inhabited place, once hitting minus 67 degrees Celsius.
Mawsynram in India is the wettest inhabited place on Earth, drenched by nearly 12 meters of rain a year.
Counting its overseas territories, France covers 12 time zones, more than any other country.
Russia stretches across 11 time zones from Kaliningrad to Kamchatka.
Spain sits geographically in line with Britain, but has kept Central European Time since the 1940s.
Samoa deleted an entire day in 2011, jumping across the date line and skipping December 30 completely.
Chandigarh serves as the capital of two different Indian states at once, Punjab and Haryana.
South Africa has three capital cities, one each for its parliament, courts, and government.
Bolivia has two capitals: Sucre holds the constitutional title while La Paz hosts the government.
A 2023 recount found Japan has 14,125 islands, roughly double the number it had been claiming for decades.
Indonesia is the world's largest island nation, with more than 17,000 islands strung across three time zones.
By some national counts, Sweden has over 260,000 islands, more than any other country on Earth.
There is a village in Norway called Hell, and it reliably freezes over every winter.
Boring in Oregon is officially paired with Dull in Scotland, and later added Bland in Australia to the club.
A village in Wales bears a 58-letter name, invented in the 1800s largely to attract railway tourists.
An Australian town is named Seventeen Seventy, after the year Captain Cook came ashore there.
Batman is a real city in southeastern Turkey, home to hundreds of thousands of people.
Kinshasa and Brazzaville sit directly across the Congo River from each other, two capital cities within sight of one another.
Rome contains an entire foreign country within its city limits: Vatican City.
You cannot drive from North America to South America; the Pan-American Highway breaks at the roadless Darien Gap.
The Sahara Desert is nearly as large as the whole United States.
Antarctica roughly doubles in size every winter as the sea ice around it freezes.
All time zones converge at the South Pole, so research stations there simply pick whichever time suits their supply flights.
No country owns Antarctica; a 1959 treaty set the whole continent aside for peace and science.
Even Antarctica has a cash machine, installed at America's McMurdo research station.
Mount Erebus in Antarctica is an active volcano with a permanent lake of molten lava at its summit.
Ethiopia's calendar runs about seven years behind everyone else's, so it celebrated the new millennium in 2007.
Selling chewing gum is banned in Singapore, with an exception for medicinal gum from pharmacies.
Bhutan absorbs more carbon than it emits, making it one of the only carbon-negative countries on Earth.
About a third of the Netherlands lies below sea level, and its main airport sits four meters under it.
No spot in New Zealand is more than about 130 kilometers from the sea.
Kazakhstan is the largest country in the world with no ocean coastline.
Lake Hillier in Australia is bright bubblegum pink, and the water keeps its color even in a bottle.
A river in the Peruvian Amazon runs hot enough to brew tea, killing animals unlucky enough to fall in.
Blood Falls in Antarctica oozes rusty red water from a glacier, stained by iron-rich brine trapped below the ice.
A gas crater in Turkmenistan nicknamed the Door to Hell has been burning continuously since 1971.
The Dead Sea is so salty that swimmers float effortlessly on the surface without even trying.
After rain, Bolivia's giant Uyuni salt flat becomes a mirror so vast and flat it is used to calibrate satellites.
Mount Thor in Canada has the greatest sheer vertical drop on Earth, a cliff face of about 1,250 meters.
The Bay of Fundy in Canada has the highest tides in the world, rising and falling up to 16 meters.
One of Andorra's two heads of state is the sitting President of France, a foreign leader by default.
Ecuador is literally named after the equator, which runs right through the country.
Costa Rica abolished its army in 1949 and has done without one ever since.
Mexico City is sinking as it drains the aquifer beneath it; parts have dropped by more than nine meters.
Jakarta is sinking so fast that Indonesia is building a brand new capital city on the island of Borneo.
Switzerland and Italy have had to redraw parts of their shared border because the glaciers defining it are melting.
The summit of Mount Everest sits exactly on the border, so a climber on top stands in both Nepal and China.
Iquitos in Peru is the largest city in the world that cannot be reached by road.
A hill in New Zealand holds the record for the longest official place name on Earth, at 85 letters.
The world's shortest scheduled flight, between two Scottish islands, can take less than two minutes.
